I can=92t= remember=20 if any one has mentioned the new book on Ella, Barbara Ransby=92s Ella Baker= =20 and the Black Freedom Movement: A Radical Democratic Vision (Unc, 2003) but= =20 I am two-thirds through it and I think it is very important.] There is some argument about Sara Evans. As I get, some of the veteran=20 SNCC women think the book=92s viewpoint is too much that of white women who= =20 came into the movement in 1964 or later, who had a very different=20 experience from those who came earlier. Casey Hayden ( co-author of =93A= =20 Kind of Memo,=94 which did much to ignite one wave of the modern feminist=20 movement) insists that SNCC should be thought of as the mother, not the=20 stepmother of the feminist movement. John Dittmer captures some of this=20 in Local People. Re Movement Chronology: Secondly, on the question of various ways of marking the "beginning"(s) of= =20 the movement, I would argue that a "critical mass" of potential activism=20 that had accrued among younger southern blacks by 1960 was set-off in a=20 chain reaction to the Greensboro sit-ins. I would certainly not seek to=20 detract from or minimize the importance of earlier periods and forms of=20 struggle. But it seems to me that Greensboro initiated a wave of activism=20 that was unique in our history; involving nearly simultaneous, related and= =20 connected if not coordinated, grassroots mobilization in communities all=20 over the south, and involving support activities in several other areas. Perry Hall This sounds right to me. In our zeal to push the =93beginning=94 of the=20 movement back in time, we should not overlook what was in fact distinctive= =20 about the post -sit-in world. At the same time, though, I don=92t want= =20 to forget Aldon Morris=92s point that the spread of the sit-in idea was a=20 collaboration of young and older activits. (Origins of the Civil Rights=20 Movement) I think Professor Lazerow is right that we may never know why Greensboro=20 sparked what it sparked (although I suspect the density of Black colleges=20 in the Carolinas had something to do with it) and also that the Sixties=20 writ large =96 the period of direct action, confrontation politics, began= =20 in Greensboro. CP African and African American Studies Program Box 90252 The John Hope Franklin Center 2204 Erwin Road Duke University Durham, NC 27708 919-684-2830; fax- 684-2832 This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. --=====================_360067800==_.ALT Content-Type: text/html; charset="iso-8859-1" Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable

Dear forum:

Still playing catch  up---- sorry.



RE the paradox of teaching about King=92s flaws:

The deeper paradox in this discussion is why people continue to assume that great leaders are also going to be moral exemplars.   Like some of you, I think it is very important to pull that assumption into the light and examine it.   It represents a kind of thinking that , I believe, has much to do with the current poor state of the national political discourse.   Re King, I think the point is that he called on us to be the best people we can be.  He had a profound faith in our moral possibilities  =96 whether misplaced is still an open question.   The fact that a flawed person from a social background that in some militated against the development of social conscience could make such important contributions is entirely hopeful.

I put particular emphasis on King=92s inability to appreciate gender inequality and the difficulty he had dealing with women on an egalitarian basis.    It would be easier to make a case that Malcolm was trying to rethink gender than that King was.  (See Hakim Jamal,
 From the dead level: Malcolm X and me, ( London, Deutsch, 1971), the discussion of Malcolm=92s last visit to LA.




RE That Speech:

The following information appears on several websites (and most of the ones I saw were pretty rightwing):

----------------------------------------------------------------------------= ----------------------------

 Archibald Carey's Speech
Republican National Convention, 1952

Keith Miller, in his book "Voice of Deliverance: the language of Martin Luther King and its sources" noticed the strong similiarities between the conclusion of King's famous "I have a Dream" speech and a speech given by Archibald Carey at the Republican National Convention in 1952. Carey was a correspondant of King's.



We, Negro Americans, sing with all
loyal Americans:
My country 'tis of thee,
Sweet land of liberty,
Of thee I sing.
Land where my fathers died,
Land of the Pilgrims' pride
From every mountainside
Let freedom ring!
That's exactly what we mean__
from every mountain side,
let freedom ring.
Not only from the Green Mountains
and White Mountains of Vermont
and New Hampshire;
not only from the Catskills
of New York;
but from the Ozarks
in Arkansas,
from the Stone Mountain
in Georgia,
from the Blue Ridge Mountains
of Virginia
__let it ring not only for the minorities
of the United States, but for the disinherited
of all the earth__may the Republican
Party, under God, from every mountainside,
LET FREEDOM RING!



I haven=92t been able to ver= ify it beyond this but if it is true, it does not necessarily represent plagiarism in the sense his dissertation did.    Southern Baptist ministers habitually borrowed sermons or parts of sermons from one another with no expectation of attribution.    Academic expectations don=92t apply to this culture.

I am more interested in the question of why that speech has come to be so iconic.  Certainly, at the time it was given, not everybody found it so special.    Feel free to call me cynical but I suspect that speech has become so popular precisely because it says so little.   Compare it to almost any speech from the last two years of his life.    They tend to be much sharper edged.   =93I Have a Dream =93   offers no analysis of what=92s wrong and no sense of what it will take to make it right.    It=92s a set of cascading images into which people can read what they want.   That makes it a wonderful speech for that occasion, an occasion that calls for unity.  I assume King was pursuing a particular agenda that day which make that day a questionable way to remember him.   There is a reason we don=92t see commercials pimping off the speeches from 1966-68.   In those years, he was condemning militarism and materialism and economic inquality.   He was always the advocate of brotherhood but he understood brotherhood to require a re-structuring of privilege.   I=92ve discussed some of this in Debating the Civil Rights Movement and Richard Lentz has a very important discussion of it.

Richard Lentz. Symbols, the news magazines, and Martin Luther King (Baton Rouge : Louisiana State University Press, c1990.).  So far as I know, this is the best analysis of how the media covered King and particularly of how , at his death, it immediately switched from criticizing him for being too militant and started using him as a way to criticize other militants.

Re integration:

This week I came across another interesting commentary on the disadvantages of desegregation  =96 if that=92s the right word =96 in schools =96 Vivian Morris and Curtis Morris, The Price They Paid: Desegregation in an African American Community (Teachers College, 2002).  It=92s basically a story of how desegregation destroyed a nurturing, segregated high school that embodied much of the tradition and communal spirit of the town it served.  After desegregation:
African American parents felt that their children were punished unfairly and were discouraged from using their talents, and that parents and their children were treated with hostility.  African American students felt that some teachers were insensitive in the manner in which they handled classroom topics related to the history and experiences of African Americans.  They also felt they had to prove their worth and and their abilities while White students did not.  And when they did, they were considered to be =93unusual=94 or as one student said a =93novelty=94 among their race.  While these students were able to survive, they never felt that they =93fitted in=94 or felt safe and secure a= s they had at Trenholm High.  (P. 11)



Thanks to ??  for the Henry Louis Gates quote.  That summarizes perfectly what I hear from a great many Black adults who attended segegregated high schools  =96 whites didn=92t have anything w= e wanted but resources. 
I wonder ,though, if comments like these reflect the whole story.    I=92m thinking there isn=92t another story, places where the resource base was so low as to prevent the kind of positive educational culture .  If memory serves, the average school expenditure ratio in 20th century south was about 3 to 1, which means there were places which it was much larger than that.    Presumably, circumstances must have sometimes conspired such that some of the most poorly funded districts, also got the most marginally qualified teachers,   school years that were only 5 or 6 months, principals who were primarily concerned with pleasing the white people who paid them, (there is a portrait of one such in the first chapter of Simple Justice.)  Most of the commentary we have about the virtues of Black education before 1954 comes from people who attended segregated high schools but I would guess that most Southern counties didn=92t even have black high schools until some time after WWII.   (Does anyone know?)   Thus, the trade-offs involved in desegregation could have been very different for different groups of Blacks.   This seems perfectly logical to me but I don=92t have any evidence to support it. The most rural place I have ever done interviews is Greenwood , Mississippi and everyone I talked to there was full of praise for the schools they went, too.   Still, I=92m not sure their sentiments represent those of the people =93out in the rural.=94


Gallup polls and consensus history

Earlier I mentioned that I like to have students follow Gallup poll trends from the sixities.   Thumbing through George Gallup=92s The Gallup Poll: Public Opinion, 1935-1971, vol. 3  (Random House), I was struck by what a good resource it would be for teaching modern US history generally.  With respect to the civil rights movement I noted a few things:

=96 In feb. 62, 32% of the public thought the Kennedy administration was pushing integration too fast, against 11% saying too slow.  This in interesting given how little the administration had actually done.

=96 in feb. of =9162, 80% of whites (86% of southern whites) thought Negro children in their communities had the same educational opportunities as white children.    In August of =9163, 56% of the population thought Negroes were generally treated the same as whites.  There was some sense that the race problem was solved, then, even before the landmark legislation of the sixties.

[I cannot help but note that in the same year, two-thirds of the public disapproved of women wearing shorts.]

=96 In February =9163, 72% thought giving federal aid to segregated schools was ok. 

- In July =9163, 60% thought mass demonstrations were more likely to hurt the Negro cause than help it.    On the eve of the march on Washington, 63% of the public disapproved of the idea.   Students often think there was some kind of national consensus about race in the sixties .   In fact, the movement was generally flying against the winds of public opinion and was perceived as being radical even doing its direct action phase.   I take this to support what Robert Newby said in an earlier posting about how =93civil rights for blacks was a heavily contested issue at the hands of the common voter.=94



RE the Cold War and Class War:

In Jack Bloom=92s Race, Class and the Civil Rights Movement (Indiana, 1987, p. 77) he makes the point that the State Department thought that 50% of all Soviet anti-US propaganda referred to the US racial situation.


I am grateful to Susan Strickland for her interesting comment:


=93In my not so humble opinion, the whole problem with class_based models of history, and in this regard Zinn, et.al, do favor the more provocative tack, is that they regard
history as huge waves of orchestrated events guided by white men in
business suits with some sort of secret master plan.  There is no way
one can read Constance Curry's book, Silver Rights, or the numerous
biographies of female civil rights leaders (black and white)and not come
away with the overwhelming sense that individual acts of bravery prodded
less than enthusiastic state and local governments to acknowledge what
all could see:  human rights were being violated.=94


        I disagree with the idea that =93all could see the injustice.=94  I think that=92s presentism.  The moral issues which seem so clear in retrospect were much fuzzier at the time.   I think that=92s a part of what=92s behind some of those Gallup poll figures.    In fact, I regard it as a characteristic of American history that in each historical era, people could see clearly the injustice of the previous era.  The larger point, though, that unnuanced history from the Left can be as useless as unnuanced history from the Right, is well-taken.    I think one of the most promising developments in movement scholarship is the work in progress trying to give us a more complex picture of racists.    On the other hand, I think that privilege ordinarily tries to protect and extend itself   =97 there are exceptions =96 and that does not require any conspiracy.    Take a look at Steven Lawson=92s essay in Debating the Civil Rights Movement ) Rowman and Littlefield, 1998, specifically, his treatment of LBJ.  He argues that Johnson was in fact more personally committed to the movement than JFK, perhaps crucial to getting the 1964 cr bill passed when it did , yet undermined the the Freedom Democrat party at a time when I think it was the most legitimate voice of Black Mississippi.   As Strickland suggests, it is not an either/or.

        I also want to empathetically disagree with the idea that  =93the politics of the cold war are separate from the reality of community based change.=94   It is true that one doesn=92t determine the other but they interact in important ways.  In order for the movement to happen at all, the Feds had to weigh in, to paraphrase Bob Moses.    Activists learned the hard way that you couldn=92t rely on the Federal response to moral or constitutional issues but from Eisenhower to Johnson, they wouldn=92t tolerate being embarrassed on the interenational stage.   This is very clear in the reaction to the killings of Schwerner, Chaney and Goodman.  Thus, the fact that activists eventually got some level of local protection is which much tied to the international situation.      The international situation

        Thanks, too, for pointing out how the role of women in resolving issues at the community level has been understudied.





Septima and Sara Evans
Cynthia Stokes Brown's "Ready From Within" offers an interesting narrative, but certainly not the level of analysis that Clark's contibution deserves.  If others can offer other sources or even current work on Clark, I'd be interested to hear about it.  Also Sara Evans' "Personal Politics" is quite successful in connecting women's work in the CRM to the later emergence of the Women's Movement.
Glenn Mitoma

Kattherine Mellon at Yale is doing a dissertation on Septima , part of which will appear in Komozi Woodard and Jeanne Theoharris=92s Groundwork: Local Black Freedom Struggles
in America, (forthcoming, spring 2004).   There is also an article about her in Women in the Civil Rights Movement: Trailblazers and Torchbearers, 1941_1965 (Carlson, 1990) and, of course, her own Echo in My Soul.   Part of what is important about her to me is that she exemplifies a tradition of humanism that grows specifically out of the African American experience in the South, as well as the self-consciously democratic element of the movement.    Whether that makes her as significant an actor as Ella Baker is a tough question for me.

[That reminds me.   Contrasting Malcolm and Martin can be very useful,   but lately I=92ve been doing more contrasting of Martin with Ella, specifically around the questions of which style of leadership has what advantages.   It can lead to some very good discussions.   I can=92t remember if any one has mentioned the new book on Ella, Barbara Ransby=92s Ella Baker and the Black Freedom Movement: A Radical Democratic Vision (Unc, 2003) but I am two-thirds through it and I think it is very important.]

There is some argument about Sara Evans.  As I get, some of the veteran SNCC women think the book=92s viewpoint is too much that of white women who came into the movement in 1964 or later, who had a very different experience from those who came earlier.    Casey Hayden ( co-author of =93A Kind of Memo,=94 which did much to ignite one wav= e of the modern feminist movement) insists that SNCC should be thought of as the mother, not the stepmother of the feminist movement.   John Dittmer captures some of this in Local People.





Re Movement Chronology:
Secondly, on the question of various ways of marking the "beginning"(s) of the movement, I would argue that a "critical mass" of potential activism that had accrued among younger southern blacks by 1960 was set-off in a chain reaction to the Greensboro sit-ins. I would certainly not seek to detract from or minimize the importance of earlier periods and forms of struggle. But it seems to me that Greensboro initiated a wave of activism that was unique in our history; involving nearly simultaneous, related and connected if not coordinated, grassroots mobilization in communities all over the south, and involving support activities in several other areas.

Perry Hall

This sounds right to me.  In our zeal to push the =93beginning=94 of th= e movement back in time, we should not overlook what was in fact distinctive about the post -sit-in world.    At the same time, though,   I don=92t want to forget Aldon Morris=92s point that the spread of the sit-in idea was a collaboration of young and older activits.   (Origins of the Civil Rights Movement)

I think Professor Lazerow is right that we may never know why Greensboro sparked what it sparked (although I suspect the density of Black colleges in the Carolinas had something to do with it) and also that the Sixties writ large   =96 the period of direct action, confrontation politics, began in Greensboro. 

CP





African and African American Studies Program
Box 90252
The John Hope Franklin Center
2204 Erwin Road
Duke  University
Durham, NC 27708
919-684-2830; fax- 684-2832 This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. --=====================_360067800==_.ALT-- ========================================================================= Date: Tue, 28 Oct 2003 23:13:20 -0500 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Kimberly C Ellis Subject: Greenwood, Mississippi Comments: cc: Charles Payne In-Reply-To: <5.1.0.14.2.20031027173047.01d91780@mail-cm.acpub.duke.edu> MIME-version: 1.0 Content-type: text/plain; charset=us-ascii; format=flowed Content-disposition: inline Content-transfer-encoding: 7bit Charles, et. al, You mentioned doing some research in Greenwood, Mississippi. I am trying to get some information on this place, perhaps the origin of the name "Greenwood" and where some of the folks who migrated from this area might have traveled to? Any help, whatsoever, would be appreciated, KCE This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ========================================================================= Date: Wed, 29 Oct 2003 07:17:00 -0700 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Stefanie Beninato Subject: Re: I Have a Dream speech MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: multipart/alternative; boundary="----=_NextPart_000_0049_01C39DEC.A51BA180" This is a multi-part message in MIME format. ------=_NextPart_000_0049_01C39DEC.A51BA180 Content-Type: text/plain; charset="iso-8859-1" Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable I have come lately to this particularly discussion. I do want to add = that for me the I Have a Dream speech did and still does send goosebumps = and shivers through my body especially when I hear King delivering it. = Perhaps more than the words were the passionalte delivery and what = seemed to be the deep abiding faith in the ability of people to change = their lives that I believe so affected those who listened to that = speech. Stefanie Beninato, Ph.D. ----- Original Message -----=20 From: Charles Payne=20 To: CIVILRIGHTS@ASHP.LISTSERV.CUNY.EDU=20 Sent: Monday, October 27, 2003 3:31 PM Dear forum:=20 Still playing catch up---- sorry. RE the paradox of teaching about King's flaws: The deeper paradox in this discussion is why people continue to assume = that great leaders are also going to be moral exemplars. Like some of = you, I think it is very important to pull that assumption into the light = and examine it. It represents a kind of thinking that , I believe, has = much to do with the current poor state of the national political = discourse. Re King, I think the point is that he called on us to be = the best people we can be. He had a profound faith in our moral = possibilities - whether misplaced is still an open question. The fact = that a flawed person from a social background that in some militated = against the development of social conscience could make such important = contributions is entirely hopeful.=20 I put particular emphasis on King's inability to appreciate gender = inequality and the difficulty he had dealing with women on an = egalitarian basis. It would be easier to make a case that Malcolm was = trying to rethink gender than that King was. (See Hakim Jamal, From the dead level: Malcolm X and me, ( London, Deutsch, 1971), the = discussion of Malcolm's last visit to LA.=20 RE That Speech: The following information appears on several websites (and most of the = ones I saw were pretty rightwing): = -------------------------------------------------------------------------= ------------------------------- Archibald Carey's Speech Republican National Convention, 1952 Keith Miller, in his book "Voice of Deliverance: the language of = Martin Luther King and its sources" noticed the strong similiarities = between the conclusion of King's famous "I have a Dream" speech and a = speech given by Archibald Carey at the Republican National Convention in = 1952. Carey was a correspondant of King's. We, Negro Americans, sing with all loyal Americans: My country 'tis of thee, Sweet land of liberty, Of thee I sing. Land where my fathers died, Land of the Pilgrims' pride From every mountainside Let freedom ring! That's exactly what we mean__ from every mountain side, let freedom ring. Not only from the Green Mountains and White Mountains of Vermont and New Hampshire; not only from the Catskills of New York; but from the Ozarks in Arkansas, from the Stone Mountain in Georgia, from the Blue Ridge Mountains of Virginia __let it ring not only for the minorities of the United States, but for the disinherited of all the earth__may the Republican Party, under God, from every mountainside, LET FREEDOM RING! I haven't been able to verify it beyond this but if it is true, it = does not necessarily represent plagiarism in the sense his dissertation = did. Southern Baptist ministers habitually borrowed sermons or parts = of sermons from one another with no expectation of attribution. = Academic expectations don't apply to this culture.=20 I am more interested in the question of why that speech has come to be = so iconic. Certainly, at the time it was given, not everybody found it = so special. Feel free to call me cynical but I suspect that speech = has become so popular precisely because it says so little. Compare it = to almost any speech from the last two years of his life. They tend = to be much sharper edged. "I Have a Dream " offers no analysis of = what's wrong and no sense of what it will take to make it right. It's = a set of cascading images into which people can read what they want. = That makes it a wonderful speech for that occasion, an occasion that = calls for unity. I assume King was pursuing a particular agenda that = day which make that day a questionable way to remember him. There is a = reason we don't see commercials pimping off the speeches from 1966-68. = In those years, he was condemning militarism and materialism and = economic inquality. He was always the advocate of brotherhood but he = understood brotherhood to require a re-structuring of privilege. I've = discussed some of this in Debating the Civil Rights Movement and Richard = Lentz has a very important discussion of it. Richard Lentz. Symbols, the news magazines, and Martin Luther King = (Baton Rouge : Louisiana State University Press, c1990.). So far as I = know, this is the best analysis of how the media covered King and = particularly of how , at his death, it immediately switched from = criticizing him for being too militant and started using him as a way to = criticize other militants.=20 Re integration: This week I came across another interesting commentary on the = disadvantages of desegregation - if that's the right word - in schools = - Vivian Morris and Curtis Morris, The Price They Paid: Desegregation in = an African American Community (Teachers College, 2002). It's basically = a story of how desegregation destroyed a nurturing, segregated high = school that embodied much of the tradition and communal spirit of the = town it served. After desegregation: African American parents felt that their children were punished = unfairly and were discouraged from using their talents, and that parents = and their children were treated with hostility. African American = students felt that some teachers were insensitive in the manner in which = they handled classroom topics related to the history and experiences of = African Americans. They also felt they had to prove their worth and and = their abilities while White students did not. And when they did, they = were considered to be "unusual" or as one student said a "novelty" among = their race. While these students were able to survive, they never felt = that they "fitted in" or felt safe and secure as they had at Trenholm = High. (P. 11) Thanks to ?? for the Henry Louis Gates quote. That summarizes = perfectly what I hear from a great many Black adults who attended = segegregated high schools - whites didn't have anything we wanted but = resources. =20 I wonder ,though, if comments like these reflect the whole story. = I'm thinking there isn't another story, places where the resource base = was so low as to prevent the kind of positive educational culture . If = memory serves, the average school expenditure ratio in 20th century = south was about 3 to 1, which means there were places which it was much = larger than that. Presumably, circumstances must have sometimes = conspired such that some of the most poorly funded districts, also got = the most marginally qualified teachers, school years that were only 5 = or 6 months, principals who were primarily concerned with pleasing the = white people who paid them, (there is a portrait of one such in the = first chapter of Simple Justice.) Most of the commentary we have about = the virtues of Black education before 1954 comes from people who = attended segregated high schools but I would guess that most Southern = counties didn't even have black high schools until some time after WWII. = (Does anyone know?) Thus, the trade-offs involved in desegregation = could have been very different for different groups of Blacks. This = seems perfectly logical to me but I don't have any evidence to support = it. The most rural place I have ever done interviews is Greenwood , = Mississippi and everyone I talked to there was full of praise for the = schools they went, too. Still, I'm not sure their sentiments represent = those of the people "out in the rural." Gallup polls and consensus history Earlier I mentioned that I like to have students follow Gallup poll = trends from the sixities. Thumbing through George Gallup's The Gallup = Poll: Public Opinion, 1935-1971, vol. 3 (Random House), I was struck by = what a good resource it would be for teaching modern US history = generally. With respect to the civil rights movement I noted a few = things: - In feb. 62, 32% of the public thought the Kennedy administration was = pushing integration too fast, against 11% saying too slow. This in = interesting given how little the administration had actually done. - in feb. of '62, 80% of whites (86% of southern whites) thought Negro = children in their communities had the same educational opportunities as = white children. In August of '63, 56% of the population thought = Negroes were generally treated the same as whites. There was some sense = that the race problem was solved, then, even before the landmark = legislation of the sixties.=20 [I cannot help but note that in the same year, two-thirds of the = public disapproved of women wearing shorts.] - In February '63, 72% thought giving federal aid to segregated = schools was ok. =20 - In July '63, 60% thought mass demonstrations were more likely to = hurt the Negro cause than help it. On the eve of the march on = Washington, 63% of the public disapproved of the idea. Students often = think there was some kind of national consensus about race in the = sixties . In fact, the movement was generally flying against the winds = of public opinion and was perceived as being radical even doing its = direct action phase. I take this to support what Robert Newby said in = an earlier posting about how "civil rights for blacks was a heavily = contested issue at the hands of the common voter." RE the Cold War and Class War: In Jack Bloom's Race, Class and the Civil Rights Movement (Indiana, = 1987, p. 77) he makes the point that the State Department thought that = 50% of all Soviet anti-US propaganda referred to the US racial = situation.=20 I am grateful to Susan Strickland for her interesting comment: "In my not so humble opinion, the whole problem with class_based = models of history, and in this regard Zinn, et.al, do favor the more = provocative tack, is that they regard history as huge waves of orchestrated events guided by white men in business suits with some sort of secret master plan. There is no way one can read Constance Curry's book, Silver Rights, or the numerous biographies of female civil rights leaders (black and white)and not = come away with the overwhelming sense that individual acts of bravery = prodded less than enthusiastic state and local governments to acknowledge what all could see: human rights were being violated." I disagree with the idea that "all could see the injustice." = I think that's presentism. The moral issues which seem so clear in = retrospect were much fuzzier at the time. I think that's a part of = what's behind some of those Gallup poll figures. In fact, I regard it = as a characteristic of American history that in each historical era, = people could see clearly the injustice of the previous era. The larger = point, though, that unnuanced history from the Left can be as useless as = unnuanced history from the Right, is well-taken. I think one of the = most promising developments in movement scholarship is the work in = progress trying to give us a more complex picture of racists. On the = other hand, I think that privilege ordinarily tries to protect and = extend itself - there are exceptions - and that does not require any = conspiracy. Take a look at Steven Lawson's essay in Debating the = Civil Rights Movement ) Rowman and Littlefield, 1998, specifically, his = treatment of LBJ. He argues that Johnson was in fact more personally = committed to the movement than JFK, perhaps crucial to getting the 1964 = cr bill passed when it did , yet undermined the the Freedom Democrat = party at a time when I think it was the most legitimate voice of Black = Mississippi. As Strickland suggests, it is not an either/or.=20 I also want to empathetically disagree with the idea that = "the politics of the cold war are separate from the reality of community = based change." It is true that one doesn't determine the other but = they interact in important ways. In order for the movement to happen at = all, the Feds had to weigh in, to paraphrase Bob Moses. Activists = learned the hard way that you couldn't rely on the Federal response to = moral or constitutional issues but from Eisenhower to Johnson, they = wouldn't tolerate being embarrassed on the interenational stage. This = is very clear in the reaction to the killings of Schwerner, Chaney and = Goodman. Thus, the fact that activists eventually got some level of = local protection is which much tied to the international situation. = The international situation=20 Thanks, too, for pointing out how the role of women in = resolving issues at the community level has been understudied.=20 Septima and Sara Evans Cynthia Stokes Brown's "Ready From Within" offers an interesting = narrative, but certainly not the level of analysis that Clark's = contibution deserves. If others can offer other sources or even current = work on Clark, I'd be interested to hear about it. Also Sara Evans' = "Personal Politics" is quite successful in connecting women's work in = the CRM to the later emergence of the Women's Movement. Glenn Mitoma Kattherine Mellon at Yale is doing a dissertation on Septima , part of = which will appear in Komozi Woodard and Jeanne Theoharris's Groundwork: = Local Black Freedom Struggles in America, (forthcoming, spring 2004). There is also an article = about her in Women in the Civil Rights Movement: Trailblazers and = Torchbearers, 1941_1965 (Carlson, 1990) and, of course, her own Echo in = My Soul. Part of what is important about her to me is that she = exemplifies a tradition of humanism that grows specifically out of the = African American experience in the South, as well as the = self-consciously democratic element of the movement. Whether that = makes her as significant an actor as Ella Baker is a tough question for = me.=20 [That reminds me. Contrasting Malcolm and Martin can be very useful, = but lately I've been doing more contrasting of Martin with Ella, = specifically around the questions of which style of leadership has what = advantages. It can lead to some very good discussions. I can't = remember if any one has mentioned the new book on Ella, Barbara Ransby's = Ella Baker and the Black Freedom Movement: A Radical Democratic Vision = (Unc, 2003) but I am two-thirds through it and I think it is very = important.] There is some argument about Sara Evans. As I get, some of the = veteran SNCC women think the book's viewpoint is too much that of white = women who came into the movement in 1964 or later, who had a very = different experience from those who came earlier. Casey Hayden ( = co-author of "A Kind of Memo," which did much to ignite one wave of the = modern feminist movement) insists that SNCC should be thought of as the = mother, not the stepmother of the feminist movement. John Dittmer = captures some of this in Local People. Re Movement Chronology: Secondly, on the question of various ways of marking the = "beginning"(s) of the movement, I would argue that a "critical mass" of = potential activism that had accrued among younger southern blacks by = 1960 was set-off in a chain reaction to the Greensboro sit-ins. I would = certainly not seek to detract from or minimize the importance of earlier = periods and forms of struggle. But it seems to me that Greensboro = initiated a wave of activism that was unique in our history; involving = nearly simultaneous, related and connected if not coordinated, = grassroots mobilization in communities all over the south, and involving = support activities in several other areas. Perry Hall This sounds right to me. In our zeal to push the "beginning" of the = movement back in time, we should not overlook what was in fact = distinctive about the post -sit-in world. At the same time, though, = I don't want to forget Aldon Morris's point that the spread of the = sit-in idea was a collaboration of young and older activits. (Origins = of the Civil Rights Movement) I think Professor Lazerow is right that we may never know why = Greensboro sparked what it sparked (although I suspect the density of = Black colleges in the Carolinas had something to do with it) and also = that the Sixties writ large - the period of direct action, = confrontation politics, began in Greensboro. =20 CP African and African American Studies Program Box 90252 The John Hope Franklin Center 2204 Erwin Road Duke University Durham, NC 27708 919-684-2830; fax- 684-2832 This forum is sponsored by History = Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for = more resources for teaching U.S. History.=20 This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ------=_NextPart_000_0049_01C39DEC.A51BA180 Content-Type: text/html; charset="iso-8859-1" Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable

I have come lately to this particularly = discussion.  I do want to add that for me the I Have a Dream speech = did and=20 still does send goosebumps and shivers through my body especially = when I=20 hear King delivering it.  Perhaps more than the words were the = passionalte=20 delivery and what seemed to be the deep abiding faith in the ability of = people=20 to change their lives that I believe so affected those who listened to = that=20 speech.  Stefanie Beninato, Ph.D.
----- Original Message -----
From:=20 Charles = Payne=20
To: CIVILRIGHTS@ASHP.LISTS= ERV.CUNY.EDU=20
Sent: Monday, October 27, 2003 = 3:31=20 PM



Dear = forum:=20

Still playing catch  up---- sorry.



RE = the=20 paradox of teaching about King=92s flaws:

The deeper = paradox in this=20 discussion is why people continue to assume that great leaders are = also going=20 to be moral exemplars.   Like some of you, I think it is = very=20 important to pull that assumption into the light and examine = it.  =20 It represents a kind of thinking that , I believe, has much to do with = the=20 current poor state of the national political discourse.   Re = King, I=20 think the point is that he called on us to be the best people we can = be. =20 He had a profound faith in our moral possibilities  =96 whether = misplaced=20 is still an open question.   The fact that a flawed person = from a=20 social background that in some militated against the development of = social=20 conscience could make such important contributions is entirely = hopeful.=20

I put particular emphasis on King=92s inability to appreciate = gender=20 inequality and the difficulty he had dealing with women on an = egalitarian=20 basis.    It would be easier to make a case that = Malcolm was=20 trying to rethink gender than that King was.  (See Hakim=20 Jamal,
 From the dead level: Malcolm X and me, ( = London,=20 Deutsch, 1971), the discussion of Malcolm=92s last visit to LA.=20




RE That Speech:

The following = information=20 appears on several websites (and most of the ones I saw were pretty=20 = rightwing):

------------------------------------------------------= --------------------------------------------------

 Archibald Carey's Speech
Republican = National=20 Convention, 1952

Keith Miller, in his book "Voice of = Deliverance: the=20 language of Martin Luther King and its sources" noticed the strong=20 similiarities between the conclusion of King's famous "I have a Dream" = speech=20 and a speech given by Archibald Carey at the Republican National = Convention in=20 1952. Carey was a correspondant of King's.



We, Negro = Americans,=20 sing with all
loyal Americans:
My country 'tis of thee,
Sweet = land of=20 liberty,
Of thee I sing.
Land where my fathers died,
Land of = the=20 Pilgrims' pride
From every mountainside
Let freedom = ring!
That's=20 exactly what we mean__
from every mountain side,
let freedom=20 ring.
Not only from the Green Mountains
and White Mountains of=20 Vermont
and New Hampshire;
not only from the Catskills
of New = York;
but from the Ozarks
in Arkansas,
from the Stone = Mountain
in=20 Georgia,
from the Blue Ridge Mountains
of Virginia
__let it = ring not=20 only for the minorities
of the United States, but for the=20 disinherited
of all the earth__may the Republican
Party, under = God, from=20 every mountainside,
LET FREEDOM RING!



I haven=92t been able to verify it = beyond this=20 but if it is true, it does not necessarily represent plagiarism in the = sense=20 his dissertation did.    Southern Baptist ministers = habitually=20 borrowed sermons or parts of sermons from one another with no = expectation of=20 attribution.    Academic expectations don=92t apply to = this=20 culture.

I am more interested in the question of why that = speech has=20 come to be so iconic.  Certainly, at the time it was given, not = everybody=20 found it so special.    Feel free to call me cynical = but I=20 suspect that speech has become so popular precisely because it says so = little.   Compare it to almost any speech from the last two = years of=20 his life.    They tend to be much sharper = edged.   =93I=20 Have a Dream =93   offers no analysis of what=92s wrong and = no sense of=20 what it will take to make it right.    It=92s a set of = cascading=20 images into which people can read what they want.   That = makes it a=20 wonderful speech for that occasion, an occasion that calls for = unity.  I=20 assume King was pursuing a particular agenda that day which make that = day a=20 questionable way to remember him.   There is a reason we = don=92t see=20 commercials pimping off the speeches from 1966-68.   In = those years,=20 he was condemning militarism and materialism and economic=20 inquality.   He was always the advocate of brotherhood but = he=20 understood brotherhood to require a re-structuring of = privilege.  =20 I=92ve discussed some of this in Debating the Civil Rights = Movement and=20 Richard Lentz has a very important discussion of it.

Richard = Lentz.=20 Symbols, the news magazines, and Martin Luther King (Baton = Rouge :=20 Louisiana State University Press, c1990.).  So far as I know, = this is the=20 best analysis of how the media covered King and particularly of how , = at his=20 death, it immediately switched from criticizing him for being too = militant and=20 started using him as a way to criticize other militants.

Re = integration:

This week I came across another interesting = commentary=20 on the disadvantages of desegregation  =96 if that=92s the right = word =96 in=20 schools =96 Vivian Morris and Curtis Morris, The Price They Paid:=20 Desegregation in an African American Community (Teachers College,=20 2002).  It=92s basically a story of how desegregation destroyed a = nurturing, segregated high school that embodied much of the tradition = and=20 communal spirit of the town it served.  After desegregation:
African American parents felt that their children were punished = unfairly=20 and were discouraged from using their talents, and that parents and = their=20 children were treated with hostility.  African American = students felt=20 that some teachers were insensitive in the manner in which they = handled=20 classroom topics related to the history and experiences of African=20 Americans.  They also felt they had to prove their worth and = and their=20 abilities while White students did not.  And when they did, = they were=20 considered to be =93unusual=94 or as one student said a = =93novelty=94 among their=20 race.  While these students were able to survive, they never = felt that=20 they =93fitted in=94 or felt safe and secure as they had at Trenholm = High. =20 (P. 11)



Thanks to ??  for the Henry = Louis Gates=20 quote.  That summarizes perfectly what I hear from a great many = Black=20 adults who attended segegregated high schools  =96 whites = didn=92t have=20 anything we wanted but resources. 
I wonder ,though, if = comments like=20 these reflect the whole story.    I=92m thinking there = isn=92t=20 another story, places where the resource base was so low as to prevent = the=20 kind of positive educational culture .  If memory serves, the = average=20 school expenditure ratio in 20th century south was about 3 = to 1,=20 which means there were places which it was much larger than=20 that.    Presumably, circumstances must have sometimes=20 conspired such that some of the most poorly funded districts, also got = the=20 most marginally qualified teachers,   school years that were = only 5=20 or 6 months, principals who were primarily concerned with pleasing the = white=20 people who paid them, (there is a portrait of one such in the first = chapter of=20 Simple Justice.)  Most of the commentary we have about the = virtues=20 of Black education before 1954 comes from people who attended = segregated high=20 schools but I would guess that most Southern counties didn=92t even = have black=20 high schools until some time after WWII.   (Does anyone=20 know?)   Thus, the trade-offs involved in desegregation = could have=20 been very different for different groups of Blacks.   This = seems=20 perfectly logical to me but I don=92t have any evidence to support it. = The most=20 rural place I have ever done interviews is Greenwood , Mississippi and = everyone I talked to there was full of praise for the schools they = went,=20 too.   Still, I=92m not sure their sentiments represent = those of the=20 people =93out in the rural.=94


Gallup polls and = consensus=20 history

Earlier I mentioned that I like to have students = follow=20 Gallup poll trends from the sixities.   Thumbing through = George=20 Gallup=92s The Gallup Poll: Public Opinion, 1935-1971, vol. = 3 =20 (Random House), I was struck by what a good resource it would be for = teaching=20 modern US history generally.  With respect to the civil rights = movement I=20 noted a few things:

=96 In feb. 62, 32% of the public thought = the Kennedy=20 administration was pushing integration too fast, against 11% saying = too=20 slow.  This in interesting given how little the administration = had=20 actually done.

=96 in feb. of =9162, 80% of whites (86% of = southern whites)=20 thought Negro children in their communities had the same educational=20 opportunities as white children.    In August of =9163, = 56% of=20 the population thought Negroes were generally treated the same as=20 whites.  There was some sense that the race problem was solved, = then,=20 even before the landmark legislation of the sixties.

[I cannot = help=20 but note that in the same year, two-thirds of the public disapproved = of women=20 wearing shorts.]

=96 In February =9163, 72% thought giving = federal aid to=20 segregated schools was ok. 

- In July =9163, 60% thought = mass=20 demonstrations were more likely to hurt the Negro cause than help=20 it.    On the eve of the march on Washington, 63% of = the public=20 disapproved of the idea.   Students often think there was = some kind=20 of national consensus about race in the sixties .   In fact, = the=20 movement was generally flying against the winds of public opinion and = was=20 perceived as being radical even doing its direct action = phase.   I=20 take this to support what Robert Newby said in an earlier posting = about how=20 =93civil rights for blacks was a heavily contested issue at the hands = of the=20 common voter.=94



RE the Cold War and Class = War:

In=20 Jack Bloom=92s Race, Class and the Civil Rights Movement = (Indiana, 1987,=20 p. 77) he makes the point that the State Department thought that 50% = of all=20 Soviet anti-US propaganda referred to the US racial situation. =


I=20 am grateful to Susan Strickland for her interesting = comment:


=93In=20 my not so humble opinion, the whole problem with class_based models of = history, and in this regard Zinn, et.al, do favor the more provocative = tack,=20 is that they regard
history as huge waves of orchestrated events = guided by=20 white men in
business suits with some sort of secret master = plan. =20 There is no way
one can read Constance Curry's book, Silver Rights, = or the=20 numerous
biographies of female civil rights leaders (black and = white)and=20 not come
away with the overwhelming sense that individual acts of = bravery=20 prodded
less than enthusiastic state and local governments to = acknowledge=20 what
all could see:  human rights were being=20 = violated.=94


      &n= bsp; I=20 disagree with the idea that =93all could see the injustice.=94  I = think=20 that=92s presentism.  The moral issues which seem so clear in = retrospect=20 were much fuzzier at the time.   I think that=92s a part of = what=92s=20 behind some of those Gallup poll figures.    In fact, I = regard=20 it as a characteristic of American history that in each historical = era, people=20 could see clearly the injustice of the previous era.  The larger = point,=20 though, that unnuanced history from the Left can be as useless as = unnuanced=20 history from the Right, is well-taken.    I think one = of the=20 most promising developments in movement scholarship is the work in = progress=20 trying to give us a more complex picture of racists.    = On the=20 other hand, I think that privilege ordinarily tries to protect and = extend=20 itself   =97 there are exceptions =96 and that does not = require any=20 conspiracy.    Take a look at Steven Lawson=92s essay = in=20 Debating the Civil Rights Movement ) Rowman and Littlefield, = 1998,=20 specifically, his treatment of LBJ.  He argues that Johnson was = in fact=20 more personally committed to the movement than JFK, perhaps crucial to = getting=20 the 1964 cr bill passed when it did , yet undermined the the Freedom = Democrat=20 party at a time when I think it was the most legitimate voice of Black = Mississippi.   As Strickland suggests, it is not an = either/or.=20 =

        I = also=20 want to empathetically disagree with the idea that  =93the = politics of the=20 cold war are separate from the reality of community based = change.=94  =20 It is true that one doesn=92t determine the other but they interact in = important=20 ways.  In order for the movement to happen at all, the Feds had = to weigh=20 in, to paraphrase Bob Moses.    Activists learned the = hard way=20 that you couldn=92t rely on the Federal response to moral or = constitutional=20 issues but from Eisenhower to Johnson, they wouldn=92t tolerate being=20 embarrassed on the interenational stage.   This is very = clear in the=20 reaction to the killings of Schwerner, Chaney and Goodman.  Thus, = the=20 fact that activists eventually got some level of local protection is = which=20 much tied to the international = situation.      The=20 international situation=20 =

        Th= anks,=20 too, for pointing out how the role of women in resolving issues at the = community level has been understudied.=20





Septima and Sara = Evans
Cynthia=20 Stokes Brown's "Ready From Within" offers an interesting narrative, = but=20 certainly not the level of analysis that Clark's contibution = deserves. =20 If others can offer other sources or even current work on Clark, I'd = be=20 interested to hear about it.  Also Sara Evans' "Personal = Politics" is=20 quite successful in connecting women's work in the CRM to the later = emergence=20 of the Women's Movement.
Glenn=20 Mitoma

Kattherine Mellon at=20 Yale is doing a dissertation on Septima , part of which will appear in = Komozi=20 Woodard and Jeanne Theoharris=92s Groundwork: Local Black Freedom=20 Struggles
in America, (forthcoming, spring 2004).   = There is=20 also an article about her in Women in the Civil Rights Movement:=20 Trailblazers and Torchbearers, 1941_1965 (Carlson, 1990) and, of = course,=20 her own Echo in My Soul.   Part of what is important about = her to=20 me is that she exemplifies a tradition of humanism that grows = specifically out=20 of the African American experience in the South, as well as the=20 self-consciously democratic element of the movement.    = Whether=20 that makes her as significant an actor as Ella Baker is a tough = question for=20 me.

[That reminds me.   Contrasting Malcolm and = Martin can=20 be very useful,   but lately I=92ve been doing more = contrasting of=20 Martin with Ella, specifically around the questions of which style of=20 leadership has what advantages.   It can lead to some very = good=20 discussions.   I can=92t remember if any one has mentioned = the new=20 book on Ella, Barbara Ransby=92s Ella Baker and the Black Freedom = Movement: A=20 Radical Democratic Vision (Unc, 2003) but I am two-thirds through = it and I=20 think it is very important.]

There is some argument about Sara=20 Evans.  As I get, some of the veteran SNCC women think the = book=92s=20 viewpoint is too much that of white women who came into the movement = in 1964=20 or later, who had a very different experience from those who came=20 earlier.    Casey Hayden ( co-author of =93A Kind of = Memo,=94 which=20 did much to ignite one wave of the modern feminist movement) insists = that SNCC=20 should be thought of as the mother, not the stepmother of the feminist = movement.   John Dittmer captures some of this in Local=20 People.





Re Movement=20 Chronology:
Secondly, on the question of various ways of = marking the=20 "beginning"(s) of the movement, I would argue that a "critical mass" = of=20 potential activism that had accrued among younger southern blacks by = 1960 was=20 set-off in a chain reaction to the Greensboro sit-ins. I would = certainly not=20 seek to detract from or minimize the importance of earlier periods and = forms=20 of struggle. But it seems to me that Greensboro initiated a wave of = activism=20 that was unique in our history; involving nearly simultaneous, related = and=20 connected if not coordinated, grassroots mobilization in communities = all over=20 the south, and involving support activities in several other=20 areas.

Perry Hall

This sounds right to me.  In = our zeal=20 to push the =93beginning=94 of the movement back in time, we should = not overlook=20 what was in fact distinctive about the post -sit-in = world.   =20 At the same time, though,   I don=92t want to forget Aldon = Morris=92s=20 point that the spread of the sit-in idea was a collaboration of young = and=20 older activits.   (Origins of the Civil Rights=20 Movement)

I think Professor Lazerow is right that we may = never know=20 why Greensboro sparked what it sparked (although I suspect the density = of=20 Black colleges in the Carolinas had something to do with it) and also = that the=20 Sixties writ large   =96 the period of direct action, = confrontation=20 politics, began in Greensboro. =20

CP

African and African American Studies Program
Box = 90252
The=20 John Hope Franklin Center
2204 Erwin Road
Duke =20 University
Durham, NC 27708
919-684-2830; fax- 684-2832 This = forum is=20 sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at=20 http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. = History.=20

This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ------=_NextPart_000_0049_01C39DEC.A51BA180-- ========================================================================= Date: Wed, 29 Oct 2003 06:52:33 -0600 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: arrow Subject: Re: Greenwood, Mississippi MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: text/plain; charset="iso-8859-1" Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit Dear Dr. Ellis, et al, I believe Greenwood is named after Greenwood LeFlore, one of the Choctaw leaders who some believe 'sold out' the Choctaw Nation's holdings in Mississippi. The majority of the tribe was relocated to what is now Oklahoma. LeFlore remained in Mississippi with a huge plantation. John Gibson ----- Original Message ----- From: "Kimberly C Ellis" To: Sent: Tuesday, October 28, 2003 10:13 PM Subject: Greenwood, Mississippi > Charles, et. al, > > You mentioned doing some research in Greenwood, Mississippi. I am trying to > get some information on this place, perhaps the origin of the name > "Greenwood" and where some of the folks who migrated from this area might > have traveled to? > > Any help, whatsoever, would be appreciated, > > KCE > > This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. > This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ========================================================================= Date: Wed, 29 Oct 2003 13:01:33 -0600 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Paddy Swiney Subject: Re: Greenwood, Mississippi MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-type: text/plain; charset=US-ASCII but had a county in Oklahoma named for him. _____________________________________ If you can't be a good example, you'll just have to be a horrible warning. -Catherine Aird- This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ========================================================================= Date: Wed, 29 Oct 2003 15:15:13 -0600 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Curtis Austin Subject: Re: Southern black perspectives on integration today MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: text/plain; charset="iso-8859-1" Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable Sorry for the delay JG, but here are more insights. Having lived in MS all my life (with the exception of 3 hot years in Florida) and being black to boot, I've had the privilege to listen to man= y people who lived through the era of segregation--so long before I took th= e job at the Center for Oral History I had a pretty good idea of how at lea= st some older blacks felt about the "gains" made since integration. As you can probably guess, the largest complaints come from school teache= rs and business owners who lost out when it came to integration since what i= t meant in real life was demotion or loss of a job/business. They therefor= e believe that if history were allowed to take its course and if things rea= lly were separate and equal that blacks could have continued to make giant strides. Since this did not happen, they blame "shady" (for lack of a better word) whites from govt. officials on down for sabotaging all the m= uch needed legislation of the mid to late sixties. As you may well know, bla= cks continued to experience voting difficulties in the Magnolia state into th= e early seventies--something not often written about but that many Delta blacks are well aware of---my mother's family was kicked off a plantation= in Yazoo county in 1972 because her oldest brother dared to vote--Jim Eastla= nd, who as you may also know, determined which federal registrars would be se= nt where so you can probably guess that they didn't make an early show in th= e county where his large plantation holdings were. But as for the desegregation issue. The older blacks in MS that I am familiar with, though they all will assure you that they"have white frien= ds too" absolutely cannot stand the average white, or peckerwoods as they sometimes like to call them, and for obvious reasons as far as they are concerned. In their minds these whites, especially business owners and political officials, attempt to portray to the outside world that MS has changed and put the past behind her, but in day to day living, in both ru= ral and urban areas, its business as usual, minus of course the voting proble= ms and "public" lynchings. They are the ones who continued to experience, l= ong after the freedom summer volunteers went home, the ravages of MS racism. Their schools never really improved, discrimination in hiring practices never really disappeared, housing issues continued to be a problem, and police brutality remained a given when it came to blacks. So in some way= s, you can say that the two races simply "put up" with each other but not mu= ch has changed significantly--well, you might point out that "you sir" have = a job at a university where it would not have been possible 40 years ago, a= nd if you can afford to you can move into one of those high fallutin' neighborhoods, but I would say to that "yes I can," but like in the 50s a= nd 60s, the overwhelming majority of us cannot. These older blacks, when they hear people boast that MS has the highest percentage of black elected officials in the United States like to respon= d: "and they still haven't been able to significantly change the black quali= ty of life" but maybe not in those exact words. They realize that the same people who ran the White Citizens Councils and worked for and on the stat= e Sovereignty Commission continue to run the legislature--and they are corr= ect when they point that out. So what they are saying to me is "I know you got your papers baby, but do= n't let them crackas fool ya, they don't like you and they never will. So do= n't trust them." All this from people who have seen relatives run out of tow= n for little or nothing and others killed for even less. They have long memories I guess. You probably read this somewhere, but blacks (at least the ones I know) in MS have one face for whites and another for blacks---= and I would venture to say that the same applies to MS whites. Several years ago while teaching at the University of Central Florida in Orlando, one of my white students commented during one of our discussions about civil rights, that his father, once, when he was a kid, whipped him for being nice to his black friend. Of course, I was appalled. But sinc= e 90% of the class was white, I decided to take the plunge and ask if anybo= dy else had had that or a similar experience. All but two hands went up---n= ow ain't that something!!! Well, since I've moved to MS, I continue to ask the same question, at the appropriate time of course, and guess what, the hands continue to go up! = I use that as a teaching tool because if you have just a few blacks in the classroom you can get some amazing discussion from this and by the time t= he semester is over, some of my rebel flag clad students have decided that their "daddy was wrong about colored people" after all--and honestly my friends, that's the most I'm willing to ask for knowing from years of thi= s storytelling that some whites from the time they can speak, are taught to see "niggers and nigger lovers" as the enemy. So all I can say is that I think SNCC was on to something when they recommended that their white counterparts organize in the place where it was most needed--their own communities. Maybe its not too late, or is that my Mississippi naivet=E9 showing again? Thanks for listening. CA ----- Original Message -----=20 From: "Jill Gill" To: Sent: Tuesday, October 14, 2003 3:58 PM Subject: Southern black perspectives on integration today > Last week, Curtis Austin wrote: > > "To the person, and I obviously have not interviewed everyone in Mississippi who lived through the movement, they say "Inagrayshon" was t= he "wust thang dat coulda eva hapn to black folk." Of course they have livin= g history and 20/20 hindsight to help them arrive at this conclusion and I imagine the children in Birmingham came to the same conclusion via real world experiences." > > Fascinating! Please share more. I'm teaching a course on the CRM in Boise, Idaho where it is difficult for my students and I to get a grasp o= n how black Americans today, who either lived through the CRM or are living with the repercussions of it and its successes/failures, assess it, its strategies and goals. You mentioned that blacks in Birmingham today are probably responding to the fact that they still live largely segregated a= nd therefore simply want equality within a segregated environment. But why = do you think that the older ones see integration as the worst thing that cou= ld happen to them. What are they saying to you off the record that are the hindsight lessons to them? Please continue to enlighten us! > > Thank you, > > Jill Gill > Boise State University > > This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site a= t http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. Histor= y. This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ========================================================================= Date: Wed, 29 Oct 2003 14:43:43 -0800 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Barbara Egypt Subject: ThankYou!ThankYou! Everyone! MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: multipart/alternative; boundary="0-2021867403-1067467423=:94413" --0-2021867403-1067467423=:94413 Content-Type: text/plain; charset=us-ascii Friends, Before the site closes I would like to thank all of you for your insightful and sensitive posts. Here in New Jersey the State Board of Education under the umbrella of the Secretary of State's office has undertaken to put in place a "new curriculum" called the Amistead Project. Unfortunately, this was tried over 20 years ago when I was teaching in Newark, New Jersey. And, except for a number of predominately African American schools, the project was not really initiated into the overall curriculum. Perhaps all of our children will benefit from this new endeavor. Thanks again. Barbara Egypt. begypt@yahoo.com. --------------------------------- Do you Yahoo!? Exclusive Video Premiere - Britney Spears This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. --0-2021867403-1067467423=:94413 Content-Type: text/html; charset=us-ascii
Friends,
 
Before the site closes I would like to thank all of you for your insightful and sensitive posts. Here in New Jersey the State Board of Education under the umbrella of the Secretary of State's office has undertaken to put in place a "new curriculum" called the Amistead Project.  Unfortunately, this was tried over 20 years ago when I was teaching in Newark, New Jersey. And, except for a number of predominately African American schools, the project was not really initiated into the overall curriculum.  Perhaps all of our children will benefit from this new  endeavor. Thanks again. Barbara Egypt. begypt@yahoo.com.

Do you Yahoo!?
Exclusive Video Premiere - Britney Spears This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. --0-2021867403-1067467423=:94413-- ========================================================================= Date: Thu, 30 Oct 2003 08:15:44 -0800 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Nishani Frazier Subject: Re: I Have a Dream speech In-Reply-To: <004c01c39e27$52192a80$0200a8c0@stebeni> MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: multipart/alternative; boundary="0-1075275027-1067530544=:42023" --0-1075275027-1067530544=:42023 Content-Type: text/plain; charset=us-ascii Please forgive the length of this email. I did want to say that I certainly appreciated everyones input. This has truly been enjoyable. I wanted to make a comment about the speech with the disclaimer that I could probably be labeled a King sympathizer. I think the problem with the I Have a Dream speech, is not the speech but the way we choose to interpret it both then and now. I think the interpretation serves a more detrimental purpose of not just creating the untouchable moral hero, but of also suppressing the conception of the hero within the everyday person. Thus, grass roots welfare organizing and unionized domestic workers can go ignored and unheard because after all they are just poor or government moochers-for lack of a better word. I think what is most interesting about the speech is its symbolic undercurrent of conversation. I think there is a difference between what black people may have heard at that moment and white people understood, what people living in that period lived and what present day persons react and use it for (the operative word here being the use of the speech). Certainly, the behind the scenes maneuvering and manipulation to "calm" the rhetoric lends itself to what is often described as a weak, if not nonexistent, critique of America and its policies. To a degree, I actually do agree with that. I think, though, that the question seems to me to be what kind of political speech is it and can you gather that information through a historian's eyes or through a literary vision of rhetoric. In other words, I believe that the politics of the speech are not in its words but its imageryà black minister style. The imagery serves as the conduit for criticism. In addition, his commentary is not made in a vacuum separate from other current events. (I can hear the groans now, so just let me jump ahead to say that in this case I think that the cigar is not just a cigar.) At this point, I will use only a couple of examples from the most oft used excerpt of the speech- although I think there are other images worthy of commentary I still have a dream. It is a dream deeply rooted in the American dream. I have a dream that one day this nation will rise up and live out the true meaning of its creed: "We hold these truths to be self-evident: that all men are created equal." Throughout the speech, King goes back and forth between Americas claim as a center for democracy and its history of slavery and segregation. In fact, in the early part of the speech he refers to the current status of black folks as still being enslaved by the "manacles of segregation and the chains of discrimination". I think this back and forth imagery along with his presence in front of the Abraham Lincoln statue serves as a dual visual critique of American hypocrisy. I think that its also an effort to link Americas nationalist concept of itself directly to the empowerment of black folks, and that until issues of equality are addressed, the idea of American democracy is a lie. I would also note that his comments are also being given in the back drop of Americas foreign policy to stymie the growth of communism (coming right off the heals of Cuban missile crisis  62, Berlin wall, southeast Asia) and set itself up around the world as the protector and bastion of freedom and democracy. This speech also falls after a June speech given by Kennedy which have the same elements of pointing to the failure of democracy at home while preaching it abroad. I have a dream that one day the state of Alabama, whose governor's lips are presently dripping with the words of interposition and nullification, will be transformed into a situation where little black boys and black girls will be able to join hands with little white boys and white girls and walk together as sisters and brothers. This, of course, is a comment about George Wallace and the questions of state right vs. federal government, which also has its base in the battle over the issue of slavery. Wallaces attitude is also being played out in Congress and the question of Kennedys civil rights bill passing. But not only that; let freedom ring from Stone Mountain of Georgia! Let freedom ring from Lookout Mountain of Tennessee! Let freedom ring from every hill and every molehill of Mississippi. From every mountainside, let freedom ring. It is interesting to note his choice of mountains here. The history of both Stone Mountain and Lookout Mountain is not simply its presence in southern states and issues of southern entrenchment against segregation. In both cases, these mountains figure prominently in the rise of the second phase of Ku Klux Klan. Both served as headquarters for the Klan, and there have been a couple of oral histories which detail the affect of seeing a burning cross on a mountain on the surrounding black communities. In the case of Stone Mountain (I cant really speak to Lookout Mountain), the Klan was still maintaining its hold right up to the date the speech was given. Even up to the 1990s, while I was there, black folks in Atlanta were still telling newcomers that Stone Mountain is a place that black folks dont go. (This may have changed due to the later election of a black mayor) Both are also the sites for confederate army strongholds - later -memorials (Stone Mountain still has a confederate museum that proudly displays the flag). I dont want to continue to go on, but I do think that the speech is distorted by todays efforts to water down and commodify Kings political and economic philosophy. As far as favorite King speeches I leave this excerpt, "What would happen to me from some of our sick white brothers? Well, I don't know what will happen now. We've got some difficult days ahead. But it doesn't matter with me now. Because I've been to the mountain top. And I don't mind. Like anybody, I would like to live a long life. Longevity has its place. But I'm not concerned about that now. I just want to do God's will. And He's allowed me to go up to the mountain. And I've looked over. And I've seen the promised land. I may not get there with you. But I want you to know tonight, that we, as a people will get to the promised land. And I'm happy tonight l'm not worried about anything. I'm not fearing any man. Mine eyes have seen the glory of the coming of the Lord." I almost feel like jumping up out my seat right now. (lol) Nishani ps I want to thank Professor Payne for his comments and serving as moderator. I think the best compliment that I can give is that I have truly been informed. --------------------------------- Do you Yahoo!? Exclusive Video Premiere - Britney Spears This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. --0-1075275027-1067530544=:42023 Content-Type: text/html; charset=us-ascii

Please forgive the length of this email. I did want to say that I certainly appreciated everyones input. This has truly been enjoyable. I wanted to make a comment about the speech with the disclaimer that I could probably be labeled a King sympathizer.

I think the problem with the I Have a Dream speech, is not the speech but the way we choose to interpret it both then and now. I think the interpretation serves a more detrimental purpose of not just creating the untouchable moral hero, but of also suppressing the conception of the hero within the everyday person. Thus, grass roots welfare organizing and unionized domestic workers can go ignored and unheard because after all they are just poor or government moochers-for lack of a better word.

I think what is most interesting about the speech is its symbolic undercurrent of conversation. I think there is a difference between what black people may have heard at that moment and white people understood, what people living in that period lived and what present day persons react and use it for (the operative word here being the use of the speech). Certainly, the behind the scenes maneuvering and manipulation to "calm" the rhetoric lends itself to what is often described as a weak, if not nonexistent, critique of America and its policies. To a degree, I actually do agree with that. I think, though, that the question seems to me to be what kind of political speech is it and can you gather that information through a historian's eyes or through a literary vision of rhetoric. In other words, I believe that the politics of the speech are not in its words but its imageryà black minister style. The imagery serves as the conduit for criticism. In addition, his commentary is not made in a vacuum separate from other current events. (I can hear the groans now, so just let me jump ahead to say that in this case I think that the cigar is not just a cigar.)

At this point, I will use only a couple of examples from the most oft used excerpt of the speech- although I think there are other images worthy of commentary

I still have a dream. It is a dream deeply rooted in the American dream. I have a dream that one day this nation will rise up and live out the true meaning of its creed: "We hold these truths to be self-evident: that all men are created equal."

Throughout the speech, King goes back and forth between Americas claim as a center for democracy and its history of slavery and segregation. In fact, in the early part of the speech he refers to the current status of black folks as still being enslaved by the "manacles of segregation and the chains of discrimination". I think this back and forth imagery along with his presence in front of the Abraham Lincoln statue serves as a dual visual critique of American hypocrisy. I think that its also an effort to link Americas nationalist concept of itself directly to the empowerment of black folks, and that until issues of equality are addressed, the idea of American democracy is a lie. I would also note that his comments are also being given in the back drop of Americas foreign policy to stymie the growth of communism (coming right off the heals of Cuban missile crisis  62, Berlin wall, southeast Asia) and set itself up around the world as the protector and bastion of freedom and democracy. This speech also falls after a June speech given by Kennedy which have the same elements of pointing to the failure of democracy at home while preaching it abroad.

I have a dream that one day the state of Alabama, whose governor's lips are presently dripping with the words of interposition and nullification, will be transformed into a situation where little black boys and black girls will be able to join hands with little white boys and white girls and walk together as sisters and brothers.

This, of course, is a comment about George Wallace and the questions of state right vs. federal government, which also has its base in the battle over the issue of slavery. Wallaces attitude is also being played out in Congress and the question of Kennedys civil rights bill passing.

But not only that; let freedom ring from Stone Mountain of Georgia! Let freedom ring from Lookout Mountain of Tennessee! Let freedom ring from every hill and every molehill of Mississippi. From every mountainside, let freedom ring.

It is interesting to note his choice of mountains here. The history of both Stone Mountain and Lookout Mountain is not simply its presence in southern states and issues of southern entrenchment against segregation. In both cases, these mountains figure prominently in the rise of the second phase of Ku Klux Klan. Both served as headquarters for the Klan, and there have been a couple of oral histories which detail the affect of seeing a burning cross on a mountain on the surrounding black communities. In the case of Stone Mountain (I cant really speak to Lookout Mountain), the Klan was still maintaining its hold right up to the date the speech was given.
