Even up to the 1990s, while I was there, black folks in Atlanta were still telling newcomers that Stone Mountain is a place that black folks dont go. (This may have changed due to the later election of a black mayor) Both are also the sites for confederate army strongholds - later -memorials (Stone Mountain still has a confederate museum that proudly displays the flag).

I dont want to continue to go on, but I do think that the speech is distorted by todays efforts to water down and commodify Kings political and economic philosophy.

As far as favorite King speeches I leave this excerpt,

"What would happen to me from some of our sick white brothers? Well, I don't know what will happen now. We've got some difficult days ahead. But it doesn't matter with me now. Because I've been to the mountain top. And I don't mind. Like anybody, I would like to live a long life. Longevity has its place. But I'm not concerned about that now. I just want to do God's will. And He's allowed me to go up to the mountain. And I've looked over. And I've seen the promised land. I may not get there with you. But I want you to know tonight, that we, as a people will get to the promised land. And I'm happy tonight l'm not worried about anything. I'm not fearing any man. Mine eyes have seen the glory of the coming of the Lord."

I almost feel like jumping up out my seat right now. (lol)

Nishani

ps  I want to thank Professor Payne for his comments and serving as moderator.  I think the best compliment that I can give is that I have truly been informed.

Do you Yahoo!?
Exclusive Video Premiere - Britney Spears This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. --0-1075275027-1067530544=:42023-- ========================================================================= Date: Fri, 31 Oct 2003 12:31:48 -0500 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Jack Dougherty Subject: Teaching Brown website In-Reply-To: <200310310451.h9V4pq1D010682@mail.trincoll.edu> Content-Type: text/plain; charset=US-ASCII; format=flowed Mime-Version: 1.0 (Apple Message framework v552) Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit Yes, I agree that this has been an illuminating conversation. Thank you to Charles Payne and History Matters for organizing it. Some readers may be interested in a new website, "Teaching Brown" with insightful essays and useful resources for rethinking how we teach about the 1954 school desegregation decision. Educators in settings ranging from middle school to the university level have offered some of their views, and we invite you to contribute your own ideas and reflections on our website: http://www.trincoll.edu/depts/educ/brown Jack Dougherty Trinity College, Hartford CT jack.dougherty@trincoll.edu This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ========================================================================= Date: Fri, 31 Oct 2003 12:33:28 -0600 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Paddy Swiney Subject: Re: I Have a Dream speech MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-type: text/plain; charset=US-ASCII Nishani, thank you for your contribution. I'm printing it out for all my survey classes.pds_____________________________________ If you can't be a good example, you'll just have to be a horrible warning. -Catherine Aird- This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ========================================================================= Date: Fri, 31 Oct 2003 14:38:15 -0500 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: Charles Payne MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: text/plain; charset=ISO-8859-1 Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable Dear forum members, =09 A few days ago, someone mentioned that it was difficult to find statisti= cs on the extent of racial inequality. I posted a query about that to the Association of Black sociologists. Not only did they produce an interes= ting list of resources, they did some interesting editorializing, too. See= below. I=92ll respond to the other postings of the last few days in a separate m= essage. CP X-Sieve: CMU Sieve 2.2=20 X-Sender: bonilla@neo.tamu.edu=20 X-Mailer: QUALCOMM Windows Eudora Version 5.1=20 Date: Tue, 28 Oct 2003 10:20:46 -0600=20 Reply-To: Association of Black Sociologists = =20 Sender: Association of Black Sociologists =20 From: Eduardo Bonilla-Silva =20 To: ABSLST-L@LISTSERV.CMICH.EDU=20 X-PMX-Version: 4.0.4.76838=20 Dear Professor Payne, Almost any textbook on race and ethnicity will provide data on the extent= of=20 contemporary racial inequality (Joe Feagin's, Martin Marger's, and Richar= d=20 Schaefer's=20 texts do a good job). Hence, you can recommend any of these texts to the=20 teachers. However, I think that we must tell teachers that at the end of the day,=20 this battle is political=20 and ideological. Even when we show our white students what we think is=20 "clear and convincing"=20 evidence of inequality and, therefore, of the continuing significance of=20 discrimination, they turn=20 around and say, "But that is because blacks are lazy, or are more prone t= o=20 engage in criminal=20 activities, or do not have the same ethics, or are stupid, or are this or= =20 that." My point: at the=20 end of the day, data will not solve this issue!!! What I do in my classes is take the fight to them! How? I show and=20 discuss the statistics of=20 inequality, but focus on showing them that their racial views and behavio= r=20 are the reasons why=20 people of color are behind whites! On the racial views, I focus on=20 documenting that their racial=20 views are screwed up and support the racial status quo. Wether one uses=20 the work on laissez-faire=20 racism of Larry Bobo, or the work on color-blind racism by Leslie Carr or= =20 Bonilla-Silva, or muted hostility=20 by Mary Jackman, or competitive racism by Philomena Essed, or even all th= e=20 work on modern, aversive,=20 symbolic, and subtle racism/prejudice, the conclusion is the same: whites= =20 have developed a new tune to=20 defend their racial interests (I call is the "killing me softly" approach= =20 to racism). On the behavioral part, my effort is to show them that their life is=20 RACIALIZED, too--from choices=20 in housing, schools, friends, significant others, etc. Do I convince my students? Most likely no, but at least they end the=20 semester "confused" about=20 racial matters which I believe is better than just reasserting their raci= al=20 views. And the political side=20 we all know: we happen to be educators but we all know that the best=20 "education" for whites is when we=20 get busy and express our collective anger in social mobilizations. Whites= =20 seem to learn faster when we=20 throw a few stones here and there! In solidarity,=20 EBS Date: Tue, 28 Oct 2003 11:32:19 -0500=20 Reply-To: Association of Black Sociologists = =20 Sender: Association of Black Sociologists =20 From: "Noel A. Cazenave" =20 To: ABSLST-L@LISTSERV.CMICH.EDU=20 X-PMX-Version: 4.0.4.76838=20 I recommend Joe Feagin's Racist America as a good source for contemporary= =20 studies=20 of racism and James Loewen's Lies=20 Your Teacher Told You as a good source for historical facts. Noel Department of Sociology=20 University of Connecticut=20 U-Box 2068, Manchester Hall=20 Storrs, CT 06269-2068 Another good source of both historical and contemporary data on racism is= =20 Paul Kivel's Uprooting Racism. NAC Department of Sociology=20 University of Connecticut=20 U-Box 2068, Manchester Hall The problem comes in sight, Eddy B..you are much too humble, as with you=20 Noel..i would have them read both of these works..then I would have them = do=20 some discovery.. Use this as a teaching moment..have them do some interviews with their=20 fellow students outside of this class..=20 First..develop an appropriate instrument..or use one of the gallop polls=20 recent ones..there are some good ones.. Go deeper then the political correct..=20 Do a content analysis of the news and other media: here are some links to= =20 get them to thinking: Children, media and race:=20 http://www.childrennow.org/media/race.html gender/race and the media=20 http://www.uiowa.edu/~commstud/resources/GenderMedia/ some books to consider: * Paul Beatty, The White Boy Shuffle=20 * Todd Boyd, Am I Black Enough for You?=20 * Toi Derricotte, The Black Notebooks=20 * Richard Dyer, White=20 * Ruth Frankenberg, White Women, Race Matters=20 * Paul Gilroy, Against Race=20 * Paul Gilroy, The Black Atlantic=20 * Herman Gray, Watching Race=20 * bell hooks, Reel to Real=20 * Eric Lott, Love and Theft=20 * Mark Anthony Neal, What the Music Said=20 * Danzy Senna, Caucasia=20 * Patricia J. Williams, Seeing a Color-Blind Future hope this helps Rodney D. Coates=20 Professor of Sociology/Gerontology=20 Director of Black World Studies X-Sieve: CMU Sieve 2.2=20 User-Agent: Microsoft-Entourage/10.1.1.2418=20 Date: Tue, 28 Oct 2003 12:00:53 -0500=20 Reply-To: Association of Black Sociologists = =20 Sender: Association of Black Sociologists =20 From: Rich Rees =20 To: ABSLST-L@LISTSERV.CMICH.EDU=20 X-PMX-Version: 4.0.4.76838=20 Several lists of very good statistics are availble on the website of the = New=20 Abolistionist Society:=20 http://racetraitor.org/naindex.html It is a little hard to find though. Go to the "Newsletter" page, then cli= ck=20 on each of the issue dates. On each one, click on "Whiteness Index" for t= he=20 list of stats. Here is a sample: Whiteness Index * Average number of months white prisoners serve for a crime: 24 months=20 * Average number of months Black prisoners serve for the same crime: 26=20 months=20 * Average number of months white prisoners serve for rape: 56 months=20 * Average number of months Black prisoners serve for rape: 70 months=20 * Percent of drivers on Interstate 95 who are Black: 20=20 * Percent of drivers pulled over and searched on I-95 who are Black: 70=20 * Number of Black men disfranchised in 1998 because of felony=20 convictions: 1.4 million=20 * Total percentage of Black male voting-age population disfranchised: 13=20 percent=20 Sources: New York Times, ACLU, Journal of Blacks in Higher Education Sender: Association of Black Sociologists =20 From: Rich Rees =20 Subject: Re: more race statistics=20 To: ABSLST-L@LISTSERV.CMICH.EDU=20 X-PMX-Version: 4.0.4.76838=20 I agree stats may not do it. Teach your white students to think about wha= t=20 it means to be white, which often includes exactly the blindness and the=20 privilege to choose not to see race or racism. Stats however can be persuasive to some and get other to consider. I just realized that not all the issues have a "White Index" with the rac= e=20 stats I mentioned. Here are some more that are especially glaring: Whiteness Index * Amount Black households earn for every dollar earned by white=20 households: 62 cents=20 * Median net worth per white family: $45,740=20 * Median net worth per Black family: $4,418=20 * Median net financial assets (net worth minus home and car equity) per=20 white family: $7,000=20 * Median net financial assets per Black family: $0=20 * Black-white gap in wealth accumulation, 1967: $16,000=20 * Black-white gap in wealth accumulation, 1988: $72,000=20 * Average mortgage rate for Black homeowners: 9.614%=20 * Average mortgage rate for white homeowners: 9.07%=20 * Amount extra that Black homeowners pay the banks: $10.5 billion=20 * Projected amount extra that the next generation of Black homeowners=20 will pay the banks: $21.5 billion=20 * Price of being Black for this generation (totaling higher mortgage=20 rates, less housing appreciation and denied mortgages): $82 billion=20 * Projected price of being Black for the next generation: $93 billion=20 * Projected advantage of being white for the next generation: $93 billion Sources: Journal of Blacks in Higher Education; Melvin Oliver and Thomas=20 Shapiro, Black Wealth/White Wealth 1995) From: "Patrick L. Mason" =20 Subject: Re: Racial Inequality=20 To: ABSLST-L@LISTSERV.CMICH.EDU=20 X-PMX-Version: 4.0.4.76838=20 Charles: See my manuscript Reproducing Racism: Lecture Notes on Reconstructing the Political Economy of Race from the Perspective of Stratification Economic= s. You may access the manuscript at=20 http://garnet.acns.fsu.edu/~pmason/Reproducing%20Inequality/Reproducing%2= 0Inequality.doc =2E This is a work in progress, so some chapters are better edited than other= s. peace, patrick l mason X-Sieve: CMU Sieve 2.2=20 Date: Wed, 29 Oct 2003 10:03:03 -0600=20 Reply-To: Association of Black Sociologists = =20 Sender: Association of Black Sociologists =20 From: Yolanda Ann Dillion =20 Subject: Re: your mail=20 To: ABSLST-L@LISTSERV.CMICH.EDU=20 X-PMX-Version: 4.0.4.76838=20 In addition to what has already been listed, I recommend the following: White Privilege: Essential Readings on the Other Side of Racism, edited b= y=20 Paula Rothenberg Constraint of Race: Legacies of White Skin Privilege in America, by Linda= =20 Faye Williams Two Nations: Black & White, Separate, Hostile, and Unequal by Andrew=20 Hacker For a shorter read (i.e. something that can be added to course=20 requirements at the last minute) the following two selections from RACE,=20 CLASS, AND GENDER: AN ANTHOLOGY (4th edition) by Margaret Anderson and=20 Patricia Hill Collins are particularly useful: 8. White Privilege and Male Privilege: A Personal Account of Coming to Se= e=20 Correspondences Through Work in Women's Studies, Peggy McIntosh (her=20 exhaustive list of of things that white privilege allows whites to take=20 for granted is very poignant). 9. Of Race and Risk, Patricia Williams (discusses racism in mortgage=20 lending--although one of my particularly obnoxious students suggested tha= t=20 she was lying about her experience). Finally, if the professor in question is at a university with an extensiv= e=20 film library, there is a tape that is part of the NIGHTLINE series on rac= e=20 relations that examines "How Much is White Skin Worth?" After reading=20 Hacker, Rothenberg, and the two excerpts from the Anderson/Collins reader= =20 and viewing the Nightline video, I have my students go through the=20 exercise of determining how much white skin is worth. All but the most=20 resistant students usually get a lot out of the exercise. > --=20 Yolanda A. Dillion=20 University of Nebraska-Lincoln=20 Department of Sociology=20 711 Oldfather Hall=20 Lincoln, Nebraska 68508=20 (402) 472-3631=20 e-mail: ydillion@unlserve.unl.edu --OR-- Yolanda A. Dillion=20 University of Nebraska Public Policy Center=20 121 South 13th Street, Suite 303=20 P.O. Box 880228=20 Lincoln, NE 68588-0226=20 (402) 472-1191=20 ydillion@nebraska.edu This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ========================================================================= Date: Fri, 31 Oct 2003 17:01:37 -0500 Reply-To: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement Sender: Teaching the Civil Rights Movement From: "Susan C. Maynor" Subject: Thank You! Comments: To: CIVILRIGHTS@.LISTSERV.CUNY.EDU MIME-Version: 1.0 Content-Type: multipart/alternative; boundary="----=_NextPart_000_0019_01C39FD0.A5CA0500" This is a multi-part message in MIME format. ------=_NextPart_000_0019_01C39FD0.A5CA0500 Content-Type: text/plain; charset="iso-8859-1" Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable Thank you to all who contributed to the wonderful discussions. I have = learned so much. Susan Maynor Pembroke, NC This forum is sponsored by History Matters--please visit our Web site at http://historymatters.gmu.edu for more resources for teaching U.S. History. ------=_NextPart_000_0019_01C39FD0.A5CA0500 Content-Type: text/html; charset="iso-8859-1" Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable
Thank you to all who contributed to the = wonderful=20 discussions.  I have learned so much.
 
Susan Maynor
Pembroke, NC
